rationale: Both titles share identical main clauses and are appropriate for PACE. Title Y is substantially more informative: it specifies that management was dynamically individualized (conveying a key clinical finding rather than merely labeling the case) and provides patient demographics (86-year-old woman), which adds clinical context and sharpens the study's contribution. Title X uses the generic 'A Case Report' subtitle, which adds no information beyond what the main clause already implies. Title Y's additional detail helps readers quickly gauge relevance and novelty, boosting appeal. The slight clarity trade-off (longer subtitle) is minor and does not impair comprehension.
loser_weakness: Title X's subtitle 'A Case Report' is redundant and uninformative — it tells the reader nothing about the management strategy or patient profile that distinguishes this report from any other LAA-closure complication case, missing an opportunity to convey the paper's central contribution.
